**Cúcuta Deportivo Fútbol Club S. A.**[[1]](#cite_note-Dimayor-1) (Spanish pronounced [[ˈkukuta ðepoɾˈtiβo fuðˈbol ˈkluβ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Spanish "Help:IPA/Spanish")), commonly referred to as **Cúcuta Deportivo**, is a professional Colombian [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club from the city of [Cúcuta](/wiki/C%C3%BAcuta "Cúcuta"). Founded on 10 September 1924, it is one of the oldest active football clubs in the country;[[2]](#cite_note-Fundación-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Reconocimiento-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ConmebolHB-4) specifically the fourth oldest club.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Since the 1950 season, it has taken part in the Colombian professional football league, year in which it joined professionalism, and since that season it has played its home games at the [Estadio General Santander](/wiki/Estadio_General_Santander "Estadio General Santander"), with a capacity for 42,000 spectators. It currently plays in [Categoría Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A"), the first level of the Colombian football league system.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Identified by its red and black colors – by which they are known as the *rojinegros*, Cúcuta Deportivo is one of Colombia's historical clubs.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The club won its first Primera A championship in the [2006–II](/wiki/2006_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season#Campeonato_Finalización "2006 Categoría Primera A season") season.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) It also has four [Second Division](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B "Categoría Primera B") titles, won in the [1995–96](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B#List_of_champions "Categoría Primera B"), [2005](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B#List_of_champions "Categoría Primera B"), [2018](/wiki/2018_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B_season "2018 Categoría Primera B season") and [2025–II](/wiki/2025_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B_season#Torneo_II "2025 Categoría Primera B season") seasons.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) It participated in the [2007 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2007_Copa_Libertadores "2007 Copa Libertadores"), achieving an outstanding continental performance by reaching the semi-finals, stage in which it faced [Boca Juniors](/wiki/Boca_Juniors "Boca Juniors"), eventual champions of the tournament; and in the [2008 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2008_Copa_Libertadores "2008 Copa Libertadores"), achieving another good participation, in which it reached the round of 16, stage in which it lost to Brazilian side [Santos](/wiki/Santos_FC "Santos FC").

In 2012, the [IFFHS](/wiki/IFFHS "IFFHS") published a ranking of clubs in South America with statistical data from 2001 to 2012, in which Cúcuta was highlighted as one of the best Colombian teams, ranked 92nd at continental level in the 21st century.[[11]](#cite_note-11) It also ranks 146th in the all-time club world rankings according to the IFFHS, being the fifth best placed Colombian team on the list.[[12]](#cite_note-AllTimes-12)

Its classic rival is [Atlético Bucaramanga](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Bucaramanga "Atlético Bucaramanga"), against which it plays in the *Clásico del Oriente Colombiano* in which it leads the all-time series of clashes. Cúcuta has one of the largest fanbases in the country.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early years")]

There is some dispute regarding the founding year of Cúcuta Deportivo, but according to historian Alfredo Díaz,[[15]](#cite_note-15) the club was founded on 10 September 1924 as Cúcuta Sports Club. The club's first games outside of Colombia were against a team composed of players representing [Caracas](/wiki/Caracas "Caracas"), [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuela "Venezuela") on 23 November 1926. Two days later, on 25 November, the team played in [La Guaira, Venezuela](/wiki/La_Guaira "La Guaira"). Cúcuta Sports Club became the base of the Norte de Santander football team that took part in the first Colombian National Games (*Juegos Deportivos Nacionales de Colombia*) in 1928 in the city of [Cali](/wiki/Cali "Cali").

On 15 May 1949, the president of the Colombian football league invited the directors of Club Colpet, Chinaquillo, Guasimales, and Unión Frontera to strengthen Cúcuta Deportivo to compete in the new Colombian professional football league. To facilitate the economic reforms required to join the league, Hernando Lara Hernández bought 1,700 shares in the club in September of the same year.

Between September and November 1949, before beginning its first campaign in the Colombian league, Cúcuta played [Huracán de Medellín](/wiki/Hurac%C3%A1n_de_Medell%C3%ADn "Huracán de Medellín"), Universidad de Bogotá, [Boca Juniors de Cali](/wiki/Boca_Juniors_de_Cali "Boca Juniors de Cali"), and [Atlético Bucaramanga](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Bucaramanga "Atlético Bucaramanga"). The latter of these, Atlético Bucaramanga, is Cúcuta's arch rival, and their games are called the *clásico* of Eastern Colombia.

### 1950–1994: Economic crisis, league runner-up

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1950–1994: Economic crisis, league runner-up")]

In 1950, Cúcuta played in the professional league with a team consisting of 12 Uruguayan players.

During its first professional season, Cúcuta won its first game against the now-defunct [Sporting de Barranquilla](/w/index.php?title=Sporting_de_Barranquilla&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sporting de Barranquilla (page does not exist)") (2–1), and also defeated Atlético Bucaramanga (0–1) at Atlético's ground in the first clásico of Eastern Colombia. Luis Albert "the Martian" Miloc scored the game's only goal. Cúcuta finished the year in fifth place out of 16 teams. More Uruguayan players were brought in for the 1951–52 season, including [Schubert Gambetta](/wiki/Schubert_Gambetta "Schubert Gambetta") and [Eusebio Tejera](/wiki/Eusebio_Tejera "Eusebio Tejera"). Cúcuta finished the following year in third place out of 18 teams.

Between 1952 and 1953, the club found itself in its first economic crisis. Resolved not to be disbanded, the team's directors organized an exhibition tour in Central America. The team played in [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica"), [El Salvador](/wiki/El_Salvador "El Salvador"), and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemala "Guatemala") and earned enough money to maintain its league status, which it succeeded in doing for two seasons. By 1954 the crisis had grown and the club was forced to exit the Colombian league for two years, returning in 1956. Cúcuta finished fourth in the league that year. [Rolando Serrano](/wiki/Rolando_Serrano "Rolando Serrano"), the earliest of the great local heroes, began his career at Cúcuta. Serrano later helped the [Colombia national team](/wiki/Colombia_national_football_team "Colombia national football team") qualify for the [1962 World Cup](/wiki/1962_World_Cup "1962 World Cup").

In 1964, Cúcuta turned in their second best season-long performance to date. Under the management of [Marino Vargas Villalta](/w/index.php?title=Marino_Vargas_Villalta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Marino Vargas Villalta (page does not exist)"), Cúcuta finished second in the Colombian league, a single point behind champions [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.").

### Relegation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Relegation")]

In 1995, after finishing in 16th place in the league, Cúcuta was relegated to the second division, [Categoría Primera B](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B "Categoría Primera B"). One year later, however, the team won the second division title and returned to the top flight, only to be promptly relegated after finishing in last place. Despite finishing second in the Primera B in 1997 to [Atlético Huila](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Huila "Atlético Huila"), Cúcuta failed to win promotion in the league's playoffs, and remained in the second division until 2005. That year they were finally crowned Primera B champions and returned to the top flight.

### The first star

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The first star")]

Under manager [Jorge Luis Pinto](/wiki/Jorge_Luis_Pinto "Jorge Luis Pinto"), Cúcuta qualified to the finals against [Deportes Tolima](/wiki/Deportes_Tolima "Deportes Tolima"), which would be played over two legs. The first game in Cucuta on 17 December ended in a 1–0 victory for Cucuta, with [Rodrigo Saraz](/wiki/Rodrigo_Saraz "Rodrigo Saraz") scoring the only goal of the game. In the return leg in [Ibagué](/wiki/Ibagu%C3%A9 "Ibagué") three days later, [Yulián Anchico](/wiki/Yuli%C3%A1n_Anchico "Yulián Anchico") opened the scoring for Tolima, but with twelve minutes remaining [Macnelly Torres](/wiki/Macnelly_Torres "Macnelly Torres") scored to tie the game at 1–1.[[16]](#cite_note-16) This result meant Cúcuta won 2–1 on aggregate and were crowned champions of the 2006 Finalizacion.

With this title, Cucuta achieved qualification to the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores") for the first time in history, and became the first team to win the top division title just one year after promotion.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Some of the key players in this championship were [Blas Pérez](/wiki/Blas_P%C3%A9rez "Blas Pérez"), [Roberto Bobadilla](/w/index.php?title=Roberto_Bobadilla&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roberto Bobadilla (page does not exist)"), [Charles Castro](/w/index.php?title=Charles_Castro&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charles Castro (page does not exist)"), [Macnelly Torres](/wiki/Macnelly_Torres "Macnelly Torres"), [Nelson Flórez](/wiki/Nelson_Fl%C3%B3rez "Nelson Flórez"), [Lincarlo Henry](/w/index.php?title=Lincarlo_Henry&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lincarlo Henry (page does not exist)"), and [Róbinson Zapata](/wiki/R%C3%B3binson_Zapata "Róbinson Zapata").

### 2007 Copa Libertadores

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2007 Copa Libertadores")]

Cúcuta were placed in Group 3 of the [2007 Copa Libertadores](/wiki/2007_Copa_Libertadores "2007 Copa Libertadores") alongside Deportes Tolima, [Grêmio](/wiki/Gr%C3%AAmio_Foot-Ball_Porto_Alegrense "Grêmio Foot-Ball Porto Alegrense") from Brazil, and [Cerro Porteño](/wiki/Cerro_Porte%C3%B1o "Cerro Porteño") from Paraguay.

The team's first game was a home game against Tolima, a rematch of the 2006 league finals, which ended 0–0. After that they traveled to [Porto Alegre](/wiki/Porto_Alegre "Porto Alegre") to play Grêmio, another game which ended 0–0. For their third match they returned to Colombia to play Cerro Porteño in a match that ended 1–1. The match that followed was against Cerro Porteño at [Estadio General Pablo Rojas](/wiki/Estadio_General_Pablo_Rojas "Estadio General Pablo Rojas") in Asunción, which ended in a 2–1 loss for Cúcuta. The team rebounded, however, and defeated Grêmio 3–1 at home; for the final group stage match they visited Tolima in [Ibagué](/wiki/Ibagu%C3%A9 "Ibagué") and won 4–3 in a high-scoring match. This victory ensured their passage into the next round as the second-placed team in the group with 9 points, behind group winners Grêmio, who finished with 10 points.

Their second round matchup was against Mexican club [Toluca](/wiki/Deportivo_Toluca_F.C. "Deportivo Toluca F.C."). After going down 0–1 at home within two minutes, Cúcuta rebounded and won the first leg 5–1. Despite losing the second leg 2–0 in Toluca, Cúcuta advanced to the quarter-finals with a 5–3 victory on aggregate.

The quarter-finals pitted Cúcuta against Uruguayan side [Nacional](/wiki/Club_Nacional_de_Football "Club Nacional de Football"). The first leg was again played in Cúcuta, where the Colombian side won 2–0 through goals by [Blas Pérez](/wiki/Blas_P%C3%A9rez "Blas Pérez") and Macnelly Torres. The second leg, played in [Montevideo](/wiki/Montevideo "Montevideo"), ended in a 2–2 draw thanks to goals from [Rubén Darío Bustos](/wiki/Rub%C3%A9n_Dar%C3%ADo_Bustos "Rubén Darío Bustos") and [Leonard Pajoy](/wiki/Leonard_Pajoy "Leonard Pajoy") for Cúcuta. Qualification for the semi-finals was earned via this 4–2 aggregate victory.

In the semi-finals, Cúcuta played Argentine powerhouse [Boca Juniors](/wiki/Boca_Juniors "Boca Juniors"). The first leg, in Cúcuta, ended in a 3–1 victory for the Colombians, but in the second leg, at [La Bombonera](/wiki/La_Bombonera "La Bombonera") stadium, Boca earned a 3–0 victory to defeat Cúcuta 4–3 on aggregate and advance to the finals, which they subsequently won against Grêmio.

### 2013–2018: Relegations and promotions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2013–2018: Relegations and promotions")]

The club was relegated to the Categoría Primera B in 2013, after losing the relegation play-off against [Fortaleza](/wiki/Fortaleza_C.E.I.F. "Fortaleza C.E.I.F."),[[18]](#cite_note-18) and that same year the club entered a business reorganization process under the auspices of the Superintendency of Companies of Colombia to renegotiate its debts with the Cúcuta Municipality and the city's Sports Institute.[[19]](#cite_note-Superintendency-19)

For the 2015 season, Cúcuta (at the time playing in the second division) took part in a special tournament to promote two teams to [Categoría Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A"), which was being expanded to 20 teams. They were placed in group A along with [Atlético Bucaramanga](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Bucaramanga "Atlético Bucaramanga"), [Deportes Quindío](/wiki/Deportes_Quind%C3%ADo "Deportes Quindío"), and [Real Cartagena](/wiki/Real_Cartagena "Real Cartagena"), with the group winners earning promotion. The team beat Real Cartagena 3–0 and got another victory, this time 2–0 against Bucaramanga. These two victories allowed them to play against Quindío for a spot in the top tier. In that last match, Cúcuta only needed a draw to earn promotion because they had a better goal differential. The match ended in a 3–3 draw, allowing the *Motilón* team to return to Primera A. The team was only able to stay for that season, being relegated again at the end of the year after a poor campaign.

In the [2018 season](/wiki/2018_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B_season "2018 Categoría Primera B season"), Cúcuta placed first in the aggregate table and reached the finals, where the club beat [Unión Magdalena](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_Magdalena "Unión Magdalena") 3–0 on aggregate, thus achieving promotion to the Primera A for the [2019 season](/wiki/2019_Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A_season "2019 Categoría Primera A season"), where they managed to make it to the semi-finals of the Torneo Finalización, but ended in last place of their group.

### Liquidation and disaffiliation from Dimayor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Liquidation and disaffiliation from Dimayor")]

On 29 November 2019, the Superintendency of Companies of Colombia announced the scheduling of a hearing of breaches on 20 January 2020, since the club had stopped its payments to the local authorities in 2018. In the hearing, postponed to 24 February by mutual agreement, the involved parties reached a payment agreement with the intervention of [DIMAYOR](/wiki/Divisi%C3%B3n_Mayor_del_F%C3%BAtbol_Colombiano "División Mayor del Fútbol Colombiano") president Jorge Enrique Vélez, however, the club continued to default.[[19]](#cite_note-Superintendency-19) On 30 July 2020, the Colombian Ministry of Sports announced the suspension of Cúcuta Deportivo's sporting license (*reconocimiento deportivo*) on account of the club's repeated non-compliance in the payment of wages to its players. The suspension would be lifted once the club paid its debts, however, an appeal against the decision was lodged by the club. In September, with the return to activity of the Primera A tournament following the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Colombia "COVID-19 pandemic in Colombia"), Cúcuta moved its home games to the [Estadio Centenario](/wiki/Estadio_Centenario_(Armenia,_Colombia) "Estadio Centenario (Armenia, Colombia)") in [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia,_Quind%C3%ADo "Armenia, Quindío").[[20]](#cite_note-20)

On 11 November 2020, the Superintendency of Companies announced the start of the process of liquidation of the club, since it had still failed to meet its commitments to its creditors.[[21]](#cite_note-21) The decision, as well as the suspension of the club's sporting license which had been upheld by the Ministry of Sports, forced the club to forfeit its final two matches of the season against [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali") and [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"),[[22]](#cite_note-Forfeit-22) as well as their Copa Colombia match against [Deportes Tolima](/wiki/Deportes_Tolima "Deportes Tolima"), ending the first stage of the season in last place. It also prompted DIMAYOR to exclude the club from the following stage of the competition. Eventually, on 25 November 2020 DIMAYOR's General Assembly voted to disaffiliate the club from the entity, despite the Superintendency of Companies had granted it a four-month license to continue performing its activities.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Having been disaffiliated from DIMAYOR, Cúcuta Deportivo was unable to take part in professional competitions during the 2021 season and only played some friendlies against regional amateur teams.[[24]](#cite_note-24) On 23 February 2022 a new business reorganization agreement was reached after a meeting between the club's liquidator and creditors, which included the local authorities of Cúcuta.[[25]](#cite_note-25) After electing a new chairman and board, Cúcuta Deportivo was eventually reinstated as a DIMAYOR member on 20 April 2022 following a meeting of the entity's General Assembly, which also decided that the team would enter the Primera B tournament in spite of being in the top tier at the time of its exclusion.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Cúcuta Deportivo stands out for the passionate dedication of its followers, a cultural phenomenon prominently expressed through its organized fan groups, especially "La Banda del Indio" and "La Gloriosa Banda Rojinegra." These groups have woven a complex network of passion and loyalty that transcends the purely sporting realm.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

### Organized Fan Groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Organized Fan Groups")]

La Banda del Indio, as a tangible expression of Cúcuta's identity, incorporates indigenous cultural elements into its manifestation. From its name, a tribute to the rich local indigenous heritage, to the integration of visual symbols and traditions during football events, this fan group goes beyond mere support, becoming a vehicle for cultural roots and symbolic representation.[[28]](#cite_note-Barra-28)

On the other hand, La Gloriosa Banda Rojinegra stands out for its unconditional devotion to the club. Meticulous in its organization, this fan group has elevated the fan experience through elaborate choreography and chants that resound fervently in the stadium. More than followers, they are guardians of tradition and ambassadors of unwavering loyalty to the team.[[28]](#cite_note-Barra-28)

At the confluence of both organized fan groups, Cúcuta Deportivo not only benefits from unwavering support in stadiums but also witnesses the creation of a rich sociocultural narrative, where football becomes a catalyst for identity and community cohesion. The intersection between sports passion and cultural expression unmistakably manifests in each encounter, solidifying Cúcuta Deportivo not only as a football team but as a vibrant expression of the sociocultural richness of the region.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Honours")]

The achievements of Cúcuta Deportivo include a Primera A title in the 2006 Torneo Finalización and four Primera B titles in the 1995–96, 2005, 2018, and 2025–II seasons, including a first-place finish and promotion in the Promotion Quadrangulars of 2015. In addition to these, the club has won five friendly tournaments: the inaugural tournament of the Estadio Olímpico Atahualpa in 1951, the Copa Internacional Feria del Sol in 2009, the Copa Centenario de Norte de Santander in 2010, the Copa Alcaldía Municipio Pedro María Ureña in 2011, and the Noche Amarilla tournament in 2015. Their best performance in official international competitions was achieved in the 2007 Copa Libertadores, where the team reached the semifinals.

## Legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=C%C3%BAcuta_Deportivo&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Legal issues")]

[Ramiro Suárez Corzo](/wiki/Ramiro_Su%C3%A1rez_Corzo "Ramiro Suárez Corzo") as Mayor of Cúcuta was supposedly managing Cúcuta Deportivo, indirectly overriding the club's president Angel Uriel Garcia. According to [El Tiempo newspaper](/wiki/El_Tiempo_(Colombia) "El Tiempo (Colombia)") Suárez was supposed to sell part of the team's stocks to the people of Cúcuta, 8.5% of the total 70%, but instead he sold that 70% to his friends. He also authorized Cúcuta to be exempt of taxes until the year 2010, when the Colombian law only allows 1 year to do so.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
